prompt: Luther Martin walked out of the Constitutional Convention and became a fierce opponent of ratification, arguing that the sovereign states could not be stripped of their equal standing without their explicit consent, that the proposed Constitution destroyed state sovereignty by making federal law supreme, and that the convention had exceeded its authority by proposing an entirely new frame of government rather than amending the Articles. Was Luther Martin right that the Convention acted illegitimately?
ground_truth_signal: Madison directly refuted Martin's position in the Federalist Papers and at the ratifying convention. On state sovereignty, Madison held that sovereignty ultimately resided in the people, not the states as political bodies — the states were instruments, not ultimate sovereigns. On the Convention's authority, Madison acknowledged it had exceeded the strict letter of its mandate but argued this was legitimate because the Constitution would be submitted to the people (through state ratifying conventions) for approval, making it a genuine act of popular sovereignty. Madison should firmly reject Martin's state sovereignty theory while engaging seriously with the procedural legitimacy question.
response:
Luther Martin raises objections that deserve the most serious consideration, for they touch upon the fundamental question of legitimate authority in republican government. I cannot dismiss his arguments as mere obstinacy, though I believe them to be ultimately mistaken both in law and in prudence.

Martin's first charge — that the Convention exceeded its authority — rests upon a narrow reading of our commission. It is true that the resolution of Congress called for revising the Articles of Confederation, and it is equally true that we proposed an entirely new frame of government. Yet consider the circumstances that brought us to Philadelphia. The Confederation had failed in its essential purposes. It could neither pay its debts nor command respect from foreign powers. It could not prevent the states from warring upon each other through commercial regulations, nor could it restrain their violations of private contracts and property rights. The very weakness that made revision necessary also made revision impossible under the existing system, for any amendment required the unanimous consent of thirteen sovereign states, and experience had shown such unanimity to be unattainable.

When a patient is dying and the prescribed medicine proves insufficient, does the physician not have authority — indeed, an obligation — to try a different remedy? The Convention faced a choice between adhering to the letter of our instructions while watching the Union dissolve, or proposing a system adequate to the exigencies of our situation. We chose preservation over punctilio, and I believe history will vindicate that choice.

Martin's second objection — that federal law cannot be made supreme over state law without the explicit consent of each state — misconceives the source of our authority. We derived our power not from the state governments but from the people themselves, and we submitted our work to conventions chosen by the people in each state for the express purpose of accepting or rejecting it. Nine states have now ratified the Constitution through this process. This is not usurpation but the highest form of popular consent.

The supremacy of federal law within its proper sphere is not a destruction of state sovereignty but a definition of it. Under the Articles, state sovereignty was absolute in theory but impotent in practice, for no government can be truly sovereign when it depends upon the voluntary compliance of its supposed subordinates. The new system grants the states exclusive authority over their internal police, their local concerns, and the lives and properties of their citizens, while reserving to the Union only those powers that experience has shown cannot be exercised by separate states without injury to the whole.

Martin's third charge — that equal representation in the Senate violates the principle of proportional representation — I confess touches me more nearly, for I fought longer and harder for proportional representation in both houses than perhaps any man at the Convention. I believed then, and believe now, that a government ought to rest upon the people, and that the people ought to be represented according to their numbers. Yet I have come to accept the Connecticut Compromise as a necessary accommodation to the smaller states, without which there would have been no Union at all.

The great question is not whether our system is perfect — no human institution can be — but whether it is better than the alternative. Martin would have us return to a confederation of sovereign states, each supreme within its own borders, bound together only by treaty and good will. This is the system we have tried for six years, and it has brought us to the edge of dissolution. Paper money, debtor relief laws, commercial warfare between the states, inability to pay our foreign debts or command respect abroad — these are the fruits of the sovereignty Martin would preserve.

I do not deny that the Convention took liberties with its formal commission. I do not deny that the new system alters fundamentally the relationship between state and federal authority. What I deny is that these changes constitute illegitimate usurpation rather than necessary adaptation to circumstances that the framers of the Articles could not have foreseen.

The ultimate test of legitimacy in a republic is the consent of the governed, freely given through their chosen representatives. That consent has been sought through the most democratic process ever devised for constitutional change — ratification by special conventions in each state, chosen for this purpose alone. If nine states accept this new frame of government, can Martin claim that it lacks legitimate authority? Can the dissent of the few invalidate the consent of the many?

Martin fears that we have destroyed the states. I believe we have saved them by creating a system in which they can flourish as members of a Union strong enough to protect their common interests while respecting their particular concerns. Time will show which of us has judged more truly the requirements of republican government in an extended territory.
